definition: Software that performs tasks we usually associate with human thinking, such as writing, summarizing, or recognizing images.
AI that creates new content — text, images, audio, or video — rather than just classifying or scoring existing data.
The technology behind tools like ChatGPT and Claude. It predicts text one piece at a time based on patterns learned from huge amounts of writing.
The instruction or question you give an AI tool. Clearer, more specific prompts produce better results.
The practice of wording and structuring prompts to get more accurate, useful, on-brand output.
A chunk of text (roughly 4 characters or three-quarters of a word) that language models read and write. Usage and limits are often measured in tokens.
How much text an AI tool can consider at once. A bigger context window means it can handle longer documents or conversations.
When an AI confidently states something that is wrong or made up. Always verify facts, names, and figures before using AI output.
Connecting an AI tool to trusted sources (documents, search results) so its answers are based on real, verifiable information.
A method where an AI looks up relevant documents first, then writes an answer based on them — reducing made-up content.
A branch of AI where systems learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed with rules.
Further training an existing AI model on extra examples so it performs better for a specific task or style.
An AI tool that can work with more than one type of input or output — for example reading an image and responding in text.
Verifying AI-generated claims against reliable sources before publishing or sharing them.
